segments.text: When Xabi Alonso was sacked as Real Madrid manager in January 2026, sources close to
Carlo Ancelotti's former coaching staff at Madrid told the Athletic that they saw
no solution right now to the side's situation.
They said that what the team really needs is a radical overhaul in the summer,
financed by the sale of a big player such as Vinicius Jr or Jude Bellingham.
If Real Madrid do look to rebuild,
where do they start and who should they look to bring
in?
They started with Tifo Football by the Athletic.
There were three areas in particular that were highlighted as needing an overhaul.
Defence, right wing and above all, central midfield.
In the backline, persistent injuries to David Alaba and Antonio Rudiger have left Real
light.
Edemilitao has also suffered another big setback,
tearing his left hamstring after last season's
anterior cruciate ligament injury, his second in two seasons.
That leaves 20-year-old Dean Hausson, who is himself battling calf issues,
and 22-year-old academy graduate Raul Asensio,
bearing most of the responsibility at the heart of defence.
The only member of the Real back four to play more than 75% of the
available game time this
season is left back Alvaro Carreras.
With both Rudiger and Alaba struggling for fitness, it makes sense to start succession
planning for left-sided centre backs to provide competition for Hausson.
In that regard, Borussia Dortmund's Nico Schlotterbeck seems a sensible option.
The 26-year-old Germany centre back is vastly experienced, with close to 150 Bundesliga
starts and is renowned for his progressive ability on the ball.
His long-range passing is excellent.
No centre back in Europe's top five leagues has completed more per game since the
start of last season, and he loves to fizz the ball through the lines with sharp
disguised passes into midfield.
In central midfield, the problem is more tactical.
Though Madrid's midfield is packed with technically gifted and athletic midfielders with
their best years ahead of them,
it sometimes lacks an orchestrator to set the tempo and stamp
authority on meandering games.
Many of Madrid's opponents in La Liga are happy to sit deep and deny those
runners space to arrive into, demanding a different type of midfielder.
One who's good on the ball in tight spaces and incisive in their forward passing to help
break down stubborn defensive shapes, a bit like Tony Kroos did before his retirement
On paper, the player within the squad best suited to such a role would be
Aurelian Ciomeni.
According to data from Skill Corner,
the France international is one of the best at retaining
the ball under pressure.
And as we can see from this scatterplot, Dani Sabaes also ranks strongly,
but the eye test
tells us that the Spaniard does not quite move the ball on quickly enough,
or have the physical capabilities to lock down such a demanding defensive role.
There's a yearning of course for a Kroos-like player,
but such a combination of press resistance,
along with an ability to distribute the ball at speed and with accuracy over large
distances, will prove difficult to find.
Some other profiles have been discussed internally.
RZ's Case-Smitt is an elusive midfielder with a knack for wriggling his way out of
tight situations with the ball at his feet, but at 19 years old,
he would need time to settle.
At Crystal Palace, Adam Wharton is excellent at moving the ball forward quickly and
incisively, but is a risk-taker who might not fit the mould.
Chelsea's Enzo Fernandes has shown that he can command the tempo of games from a
deeper role, but the Argentina midfielder would likely come with a sizeable price tag.
With Manchester City's Rodri and Martin Zubamendi of Arsenal firmly off the market,
it will not be an easy search.
But of course it's up front where the highest profile conundrum is.
Galacticos, Vinicius Jr, Rodrigo and Kylian Mbappe all prefer to play on the left.
Mbappe has settled into more of a striker's role,
rotating in and out of the number 9
position to devastating effect, but questions remain over the future of Vinicius Jr and
Rodrigo, who's usually the player shifted over to the right to give the front three
more balance.
Rodrigo has been more lively from the left throughout his Madrid career, generating more
combined expected goals and expected assists per 90 from that flank,
while attempting far more
take-ons on his favoured side.
This suggests that, should Vinicius Jr leave,
Madrid's replacement could lie within their ranks.
But that would leave the right side looking light,
with only Franco Mastantuono and Brahim
available to step up.
It could be an opportunity to look for a prime-age winger to rotate with
Brahim while Mastantuono develops.
Roma's Matthias Sula is two-footed, creative, hard-working and likes to drop into those
pockets of space where Nicol Paz may roam next season if he returns from Italian
side Como.
Wolfsburg's Mohamed Amora is lightning quick and a fine finisher.
He works hard to harass
from the front and ensure that high-pressing systems don't fall apart.
It's in Madrid's nature to target the biggest names in international football,
but spreading their funds across the team and recruiting those all-important players –
the
unflappable holding midfielder, the commanding left-footed centre-back and the energetic
right-sided winger – could help fix some of the gaps that have emerged in their
superstar squad.
